issues.title: Atlantic Avenue Mixed-Use Plan Approved — 4,600 New Units, 1,900 Affordable
issues.summary:
NEW (April 2026): The New York City Council voted to approve the Atlantic Avenue Mixed Use Plan (AAMUP), a major rezoning spanning Council Members Crystal Hudson and Chi Ossé's districts in Brooklyn. The plan will deliver 4,600 new units of housing, approximately 1,900 of which will be permanently affordable. The rezoning covers a corridor along Atlantic Avenue in the Fort Greene, Clinton Hill, Prospect Heights, and Crown Heights neighborhoods. This is one of the largest neighborhood rezonings in NYC in recent years and a major test of the City of Yes housing framework in practice. The plan includes provisions for community facilities, commercial space, and open space requirements alongside the residential development.

**May 24 update:** ⚡ MAJOR: Mayor Mamdani's administration announced on May 20 its first comprehensive neighborhood plans — the 'South of Prospect Plan' targets rezoning along McDonald Avenue and Coney Island Avenue corridors south of Prospect Park in Brooklyn. The plan will allow taller buildings and more housing along these transit-rich corridors, including near the proposed Interborough Express (IBX) light rail. Community engagement begins this summer with zoning concepts expected in 2027. This is the Mamdani administration's first major rezoning initiative and continues the pro-housing trajectory that began with the Atlantic Avenue plan. Additionally, Governor Hochul announced an RFP for 300 new housing units on underutilized MTA land in Crown Heights — made possible by the AAMUP rezoning.

**May 25 update:** City Council approved a rezoning at 2510 Coney Island Avenue in Brooklyn with modifications to zoning districts and removal of MIH option one — creating approximately 27 apartments (9 affordable). Additionally, the Brownsville Neighborhood Community Plan (NCP) is advancing, with a proposal to develop three new affordable housing buildings on vacant city-owned lots in Brownsville, Brooklyn. CM Shahana Hanif (District 39 — Kensington, Windsor Terrace, Park Slope, Gowanus) published a Q&A with PoliticsNY on May 19, and the city is opening a long-delayed Brooklyn preschool after years of advocacy. The South of Prospect Plan community engagement begins this summer.

**May 26 update:** NYC Council Subcommittee on Zoning and Franchises meets TOMORROW (May 27, 11am, 250 Broadway 8th Floor) — monitoring for any Brooklyn-related zoning items. The South of Prospect Plan community engagement begins this summer. The Brownsville NCP affordable housing proposal continues advancing. City of Yes implementation continues showing results: 23% more housing permits in year one.

**May 27 update:** ⚡ MAJOR: Mayor Mamdani released "Block by Block: The Housing Plan for a New Era" — a sweeping housing agenda committing to 200,000 new affordable rent-stabilized homes and 200,000 preserved units, backed by $22 billion in capital investment over 5 years. Key provisions: doubling the Open Door homeownership program, a new "Our Home" permanent affordable co-op program, the Construction Justice Act ($40/hr minimum wage for city-financed projects), and SPEED reforms cutting affordable housing timelines by 8 months to 2 years. Separately, the administration announced the "South of Prospect" Neighborhood Plan — Mamdani's first major rezoning — targeting McDonald Avenue and Coney Island Avenue corridors south of Prospect Park for taller buildings and more housing, preparing for the MTA's future Interborough Express (IBX). The Zoning Subcommittee met today at 11am.

⚡ MAY 28 UPDATE: Mayor Mamdani's 'Block by Block' housing plan (released May 27) provides massive executive-branch momentum. $22B capital investment over 5 years. 200,000 new affordable rent-stabilized homes. $5.6B NYCHA investment — the largest city investment in public housing in recent history. SPEED reforms cut affordable housing development timelines by 8 months. South of Prospect rezoning announced (McDonald & Coney Island avenues). The plan also doubles the Open Door affordable homeownership program and launches 'Our Home' permanently affordable co-op program. Construction Justice Act included. This is the most ambitious municipal housing plan in US history.

**June 3 update:** The South of Prospect Plan community engagement begins this summer — Mayor Mamdani's first major rezoning targeting McDonald Avenue and Coney Island Avenue corridors south of Prospect Park. City of Yes implementation showing results: 23% more housing permits in year one. Governor Hochul announced RFP for 300 new housing units on underutilized MTA land in Crown Heights — made possible by the AAMUP rezoning. An application for East 98th Street rezoning in East Flatbush (Community District 17) has a public scoping session scheduled for June 11 at 2:00 PM. The NYC June 23 primary election approaches — multiple Brooklyn council and state legislative seats on the ballot.

**June 3 update:** The South of Prospect Plan community engagement begins this summer — Mayor Mamdani's first major rezoning targeting McDonald Avenue and Coney Island Avenue corridors south of Prospect Park. City of Yes implementation showing results: 23% more housing permits in year one. Governor Hochul announced RFP for 300 new housing units on underutilized MTA land in Crown Heights — made possible by the AAMUP rezoning. An application for East 98th Street rezoning in East Flatbush (Community District 17) has a public scoping session scheduled for June 11 at 2:00 PM. The NYC June 23 primary election approaches — multiple Brooklyn council and state legislative seats on the ballot.

**June 4 update:** City of Yes 12 months in — NYC seeing 23% more new homes. 12,000+ units in pipeline from office conversions including 3,000+ affordable. Park Slope and Prospect Heights seeing largest increases in allowable square footage under City of Yes.

**June 7 update:** City of Yes ADU program update: 98 homeowner applications received across Brooklyn, Bronx, Staten Island, and Queens, with half submitted in the last two months. Application deadline: June 12 (5 DAYS). East New York's rezoning is being cited as a national model — new construction boom without gentrification, thanks to mandatory inclusionary housing. The Brownsville Neighborhood Community Plan continues advancing with three new affordable housing buildings proposed on vacant city-owned lots.

**June 9 update:** City of Yes ADU application deadline is June 12 — 3 DAYS AWAY. 98 homeowner applications received so far across Brooklyn, Bronx, Staten Island, and Queens, with half submitted in the last two months. East 98th Street rezoning public scoping session in East Flatbush (Community District 17) is June 11 (2 DAYS) at 2 PM. The South of Prospect Plan community engagement begins this summer. City of Yes continues delivering: 23% more housing permits in year one, 12,000+ units in pipeline from office conversions including 3,000+ affordable.

**June 10 update:** City of Yes ADU application deadline June 12 — 2 DAYS AWAY. ⚡ 3,100+ homeowners have applied since March reopening (up dramatically from 98 when previously tracked). Up to $395,000 per homeowner ($175K grant + $220K loan). Pre-Approved Plan Library with 11 DOB-reviewed designs launched — though only ~12% of residential lots actually meet zoning/size requirements. East 98th Street rezoning scoping session TOMORROW (June 11) at 2 PM in East Flatbush — 972 apartments across 9 buildings (14-15 stories), 194-292 affordable units under MIH. Q1 2026 housing permits nearly DOUBLED: 28,773 units filed vs 14,338/quarter average in 2025 — Manhattan up 289%. City of Yes is accelerating production citywide.

**June 11 update:** ⚡ East 98th Street rezoning scoping session is TODAY at 2 PM. The project by Midyan Gate Realty (Bawabeh Holdings) would rezone six sites in East Flatbush from C8-2 to C4-4/C4-4D — enabling residential uses currently prohibited. Updated scope: 786 dwelling units across the project area (revised from earlier 972-unit figure), with 20-30% (157-236 units) permanently affordable under Mandatory Inclusionary Housing. City of Yes ADU application deadline is TOMORROW (June 12) — 3,100+ homeowners have applied since March reopening. Up to $395,000 per homeowner ($175K grant + $220K loan). Pre-Approved Plan Library with 11 DOB-reviewed designs available. Q1 2026 housing permits nearly doubled: 28,773 units filed vs 14,338/quarter average in 2025. Early voting starts June 13 (2 DAYS).

**June 12 update:** ⚡ City of Yes Plus One ADU Program intake survey deadline is TODAY (June 12). 3,100+ homeowners have applied since March reopening. Up to $395,000 per homeowner ($175K forgivable grant + $220K low-interest loan). Funding reviewed on rolling basis — early applicants have structural advantage as pipeline fills before funding is fully allocated. East 98th Street rezoning scoping session held yesterday (June 11) at 2 PM in East Flatbush — DEIS required; 786 dwelling units across six sites, 157-236 permanently affordable under MIH. Early voting starts TOMORROW (June 13) — Brooklyn council seats on the ballot will shape housing policy. Q1 2026 housing permits nearly doubled: 28,773 units filed vs 14,338/quarter average in 2025.

**June 13 update:** City of Yes Plus One ADU Program intake survey deadline PASSED yesterday (June 12). 3,100+ homeowners applied since March reopening — up to $395,000 per homeowner ($175K forgivable grant + $220K low-interest loan). Funding reviewed on rolling basis. ⚡ EARLY VOTING STARTS TODAY — polls open 9 AM - 5 PM. Primary June 23 (10 DAYS). Brooklyn council seats on the ballot will shape housing policy. Q1 2026 housing permits nearly doubled: 28,773 units filed vs 14,338/quarter average in 2025. East 98th Street rezoning DEIS required — 786 units across six sites, 157-236 permanently affordable under MIH.

**June 14 update:** ⚡ EARLY VOTING DAY 2 — polls open 9 AM - 5 PM (Sunday). Primary June 23 (9 DAYS). Early voting continues through June 21. Brooklyn council seats on the ballot will shape housing policy implementation. City of Yes ADU Program deadline passed June 12 — 3,100+ homeowners applied. Q1 2026 housing permits nearly doubled: 28,773 units filed vs 14,338/quarter average in 2025. East 98th Street rezoning DEIS required — 786 units across six sites.

**June 15 update:** ⚡ EARLY VOTING DAY 3 — Monday, polls 9 AM - 5 PM; Tue-Wed extended 10 AM - 8 PM. Primary June 23 (8 DAYS). Brooklyn council seats on the ballot will shape housing policy implementation. City of Yes ADU Program: 3,100+ homeowner applications received (deadline was June 12). Q1 2026 housing permits nearly doubled vs 2025 average.

**June 16 update:** ⚡ EARLY VOTING DAY 4 — extended hours 10 AM - 8 PM. 44,000+ voted citywide through Day 3; Brooklyn: 11,438 check-ins. Primary June 23 (7 DAYS). Brooklyn council seats on the ballot will shape housing policy implementation. City of Yes ADU Program: 3,100+ homeowner applications received (deadline was June 12). Q1 2026 housing permits nearly doubled vs 2025 average — 28,773 units filed.

**June 17 update:** ⚡ EARLY VOTING DAY 5 — polls 10 AM - 8 PM. 62,244 voted citywide through Day 4; Brooklyn: 16,762 check-ins. Primary June 23 (6 DAYS). Brooklyn council seats on the ballot will shape housing policy implementation. City of Yes ADU Program: 3,100+ homeowner applications received. Q1 2026 housing permits nearly doubled vs 2025 average — 28,773 units filed.

**June 18 update:** ⚡ EARLY VOTING DAY 6 — polls 8 AM - 4 PM. 77,000+ voted citywide through Day 5; Brooklyn: 21,675 check-ins. Primary June 23 (5 DAYS). Brooklyn council seats on the ballot will shape housing policy. City of Yes ADU Program: 3,100+ homeowner applications received. Q1 2026 housing permits nearly doubled vs 2025 — 28,773 units filed.

**June 19 update:** Early voting Day 7 (Juneteenth). 92,000+ voted citywide through Day 6; Brooklyn 26,464. Primary June 23 (4 DAYS). Brooklyn council seats on the ballot shape housing policy going forward.

**June 22 update:** Early voting Day 10 (final day). 110,000+ estimated voters citywide. Primary is TOMORROW (June 23) — polls open 6 AM - 9 PM. Brooklyn council seats on the ballot shape housing policy going forward. South of Prospect Plan community engagement begins this summer.

**June 23 update:** ⚡ PRIMARY ELECTION DAY — polls open 6 AM - 9 PM. Over 275,000 New Yorkers cast ballots during early voting. Brooklyn led early voting with 30,000+ check-ins. CD-7 (Velázquez open seat) is the marquee race: Assembly Member Claire Valdez (DSA) leads at 81% on prediction markets vs Brooklyn Borough President Antonio Reynoso (21%). Council Member Julie Won and newcomer Vichal Kumar also running. Valdez endorsed by Mayor Mamdani; Reynoso endorsed by retiring Rep. Velázquez. Results expected tonight. Multiple Brooklyn City Council seats on the ballot will shape housing policy implementation. South of Prospect Plan community engagement begins this summer.

**June 28 update:** Atlantic Avenue Mixed-Use Plan implementation advancing — 4,600 new units including 1,900 affordable approved. Monitor construction timeline and community benefits agreement compliance.

**June 29 update:** East New York rezoning producing results — research confirms new housing lessens rent increases and slows gentrification. Mandatory Inclusionary Housing producing affordable units as designed. 840-ft tower (1,263 units including 325 affordable under MIH) approved. Brooklyn waterfront towers in Greenpoint advancing — developer committed to 662 affordable of 1,150 total units. Citywide zoning reform estimated to create 82,000+ new homes with $5B City for All housing plan.

**June 30 update:** ⚡ Monitor Point rezoning committee-approved June 25 — 1,324 total units with 50% affordable (662 units), up from original 25%. Includes supportive housing, deeply affordable senior units, 100+ units for formerly homeless. Council secured commitment to complete Bushwick Inlet Park. Returns to City Planning Commission before full Council vote. South of Prospect Plan announced: Mayor Mamdani advancing rezoning of Coney Island Ave and McDonald Ave corridors south of Prospect Park — transit-oriented mixed-use development tied to future IBX (Interborough Express). East New York rezoning confirmed producing results with less gentrification. Citywide zoning reform estimated 82,000+ new homes with $5B City for All housing plan.

**July 1 update:** Monitor Point: Land Use Committee approved 9-0 on June 25 — full Council vote expected early July. Negotiated affordability up from 25% to 50%: 662 permanently affordable units (329 at 40-60% AMI, 172 at 80-125% AMI, 161 senior units at 30-50% AMI, ~110 supportive housing). Community benefits include Bushwick Inlet Park completion ($300K/year maintenance), new Greenpoint Monitor Museum, Nassau G train ADA upgrade (~$60M), 1+ acre waterfront open space. ⚡ NYC Council adopted $115.9B FY2026 budget June 30 — includes $4B capital + $1B expense for City for All housing commitments. South of Prospect Plan: community engagement via neighborhood survey underway; draft zoning proposal expected 2027.

public_comment.instructions: Rezoning approved. Monitor implementation — first development applications expected within 6-12 months.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: This is the YIMBY proof point for Brooklyn — 4,600 new units including 1,900 permanently affordable is exactly the kind of supply expansion that lowers housing costs. The Atlantic Avenue corridor is in your 11201 orbit. This rezoning demonstrates that City of Yes-era housing policy can deliver at scale. Support implementation and push back against inevitable delay tactics from opponents.

issues.title: Council Legislation Would Ban NYPD from Equipping Robots with Weapons
issues.summary:
NEW (May 2, 2026): A bill introduced in the City Council would ban the NYPD from using armed robots as a tool for policing. The legislation would prevent the NYPD from deploying robots with weapons and prohibit the use of robots in ways that cause physical injury to people. This follows national debates about police use of technology, including the NYPD's deployment of Boston Dynamics' Digidog robot for surveillance. The bill reflects growing tension between police technology adoption and civil liberties concerns — a pattern also seen in Austin's TRUST Act (April 2026) banning facial recognition and regulating surveillance technology.

**June 22 update:** No new developments. Bill remains in committee. Monitor council.nyc.gov for hearing schedule.

**June 28 update:** Council legislation banning NYPD robot weaponization advancing through committee process. Monitor for full council vote date.

public_comment.instructions: Bill in committee. Public hearings expected. Monitor council.nyc.gov for hearing schedule.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: As a tech-oriented effective altruist, this bill is at the intersection of your key interests — technology governance, civil liberties, and evidence-based policy. Armed police robots represent a meaningful shift in the relationship between state power and technology. The utilitarian calculus here is complex: does robot deployment reduce human casualties in dangerous situations, or does it lower the threshold for force? Worth monitoring the committee hearings and supporting evidence-based analysis of robot policing outcomes.

issues.title: Speaker Julie Menin Elected — Small Lots Housing Reform Proposed
issues.summary:
UPDATE (May 2026): The New York City Council elected Julie Menin as Speaker during its 2026 Charter Meeting. Menin has proposed reforms to unlock up to 35,000 units of housing on small lots across New York City. The small lots reform targets the thousands of undersized parcels across the five boroughs that currently can't be developed under existing zoning — regulatory dead zones that prevent housing from being built even where it's desperately needed. If enacted, the reform would be one of the largest single housing production measures since City of Yes. Menin's elevation signals continued momentum for the pro-housing legislative agenda at the Council level.

**May 27 update:** Mayor Mamdani's "Block by Block" housing plan adds executive-branch momentum to Speaker Menin's legislative agenda. The plan's $22 billion capital investment, SPEED reforms, and Construction Justice Act create the policy infrastructure for Menin's small lots reform and other Council housing initiatives to deliver at scale.

**June 3 update:** The Council Advisory Group on Housing Affordability has been formed — co-chaired by Gary LaBarbera (Building Trades Council president), Barika Williams (ANHD executive director), and James Simmons III (Asland Capital Partners CEO). The advisory group will refine proposals to boost and preserve housing. The small lots reform targets 2,850 identified lots that could unlock up to 35,000 new homes including thousands of affordable units — all without costly rezonings. The Council hasn't set a legislative timeline and says it's in early talks with the Mamdani administration. Combined with the Block by Block housing plan ($22B capital) and SPEED reforms, the policy infrastructure for delivering at scale is being built.

**June 3 update:** The Council Advisory Group on Housing Affordability has been formed — co-chaired by Gary LaBarbera (Building Trades Council president), Barika Williams (ANHD executive director), and James Simmons III (Asland Capital Partners CEO). The advisory group will refine proposals to boost and preserve housing. The small lots reform targets 2,850 identified lots that could unlock up to 35,000 new homes including thousands of affordable units — all without costly rezonings. The Council hasn't set a legislative timeline and says it's in early talks with the Mamdani administration. Combined with the Block by Block housing plan ($22B capital) and SPEED reforms, the policy infrastructure for delivering at scale is being built.

**June 22 update:** No new developments. Small lots reform and Council Advisory Group on Housing Affordability continue early-stage discussions with Mamdani administration. No legislative timeline set.

**June 28 update:** ⚡ Speaker Menin proposed small lots housing reform that could unlock up to 35,000 units of housing across NYC by reforming zoning for undersized parcels. Monitor for committee hearings and vote schedule.

public_comment.instructions: Legislative proposal. Monitor council.nyc.gov for formal introduction and hearing schedule.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: Speaker Menin's small lots reform could unlock 35,000 units of housing — a massive supply-side intervention. This is the kind of regulatory reform that YIMBYs champion: removing artificial barriers to housing production without requiring public subsidy. The fact that the new Speaker is leading with housing reform signals that the City of Yes momentum hasn't stalled. Worth tracking the legislative timeline and supporting the reform through public comment when hearings are scheduled.

issues.title: Minimum Wage Amendment — NYC Council Legislation
issues.summary:
NEW (May 2026): The City Council has introduced Int 0757-2026, a Local Law to amend the administrative code of the city of New York in relation to the establishment of the minimum wage. Details of the specific wage levels proposed are pending committee review. NYC's current minimum wage is $16/hour (set by state law). Any city-level adjustment would need to navigate state preemption concerns. The bill reflects ongoing cost-of-living pressures in the city — particularly relevant as rents remain among the highest in the nation despite recent national moderation in some metro areas.

**June 22 update:** No new developments. Bill remains in committee.

**June 28 update:** Int 0757-2026 minimum wage amendment advancing through council. Monitor for committee action and vote schedule.

public_comment.instructions: Bill referred to committee. Monitor legistar.council.nyc.gov for hearing dates.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: Minimum wage policy has complex effects from an EA perspective — evidence on employment impacts is mixed but generally shows modest effects at moderate increases. The bigger question is whether NYC's cost-of-living crisis is better addressed through wage floors or housing supply expansion (the answer is both, but housing supply has higher leverage). Worth monitoring but lower priority than housing reform.

issues.title: FIFA World Cup 2026 — Council Advances Small Business Opportunity Package
issues.summary:
NEW (May 2026): The New York City Council introduced a package of legislation advancing a citywide strategy to prepare for the 2026 FIFA World Cup and ensure the global tournament delivers meaningful economic opportunities for small businesses and New Yorkers across all five boroughs. New York/New Jersey is a host metro area for the tournament. The legislation focuses on street vendor permits, small business support, transportation planning, and public safety coordination. The World Cup is expected to bring hundreds of thousands of visitors to the metro area, with games at MetLife Stadium in New Jersey and fan zones planned across NYC.

**June 22 update:** No new developments. World Cup preparations continue — tournament begins June 2026.

**June 28 update:** ⚡ Council advanced package of legislation for citywide strategy to prepare for 2026 FIFA World Cup — ensuring economic opportunities for small businesses and New Yorkers across all five boroughs. Tournament approaching rapidly.

public_comment.instructions: Legislative package in development. Monitor council.nyc.gov for details.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: The World Cup is a short-term economic event — interesting from an urban planning perspective (how does a city handle massive temporary population surges?) but low long-term policy impact. The small business and vendor permitting reforms could have lasting benefits if they lead to permanent regulatory simplification. Low priority unless you're interested in the event planning and logistics lens.

issues.title: City of Yes Housing Implementation — Tracking Progress Across Brooklyn
issues.governing_body: New York City Council / Dept. of City Planning
issues.summary:
ONGOING (May 2026): New York City's landmark City of Yes for Housing Opportunity zoning reform — adopted in late 2024 — continues implementation across all five boroughs. The reform eliminated parking mandates citywide, allowed accessory dwelling units (ADUs) on most residential lots, and created new pathways for affordable housing production. In Brooklyn, the Atlantic Avenue Mixed-Use Plan (approved April 2026, 4,600 units) is the first major test of the post-City of Yes framework at neighborhood scale. Citywide, the reform is projected to add 80,000+ new housing units over the next 15 years. Implementation challenges include navigating community board opposition, securing construction financing in a high-interest-rate environment, and ensuring that new development includes genuinely affordable units rather than just market-rate luxury housing.

**May 19 update:** NYC has seen 23% more new housing permits in the first year of City of Yes compared to the prior year. Office-to-residential conversions are a major driver — more than 12,000 homes are currently in the pipeline from office conversions, including over 3,000 permanently affordable units, streamlined by City of Yes reforms and the state's new 467-M tax incentive. Park Slope and Prospect Heights in Brooklyn have seen among the largest increases in maximum allowable square footage under City of Yes.

**May 27 update:** Mayor Mamdani's "Block by Block" housing plan provides the most ambitious policy framework yet for City of Yes implementation — $22 billion in capital investment over 5 years, 200,000 new affordable rent-stabilized homes, and SPEED reforms cutting affordable housing timelines by 8 months to 2 years. The plan's doubling of the Open Door homeownership program and new "Our Home" co-op program complement City of Yes's supply-side zoning reforms with direct affordability tools. The South of Prospect Neighborhood Plan — Mamdani's first major rezoning along McDonald and Coney Island avenues — will test how City of Yes and neighborhood-level planning interact to produce housing at scale.

⚡ MAY 28 UPDATE: Mamdani's 'Block by Block' plan (May 27) supercharges City of Yes implementation. $22B capital over 5 years provides dedicated funding pipeline. SPEED reforms cutting affordable timelines by 8 months directly address the City of Yes permitting bottleneck. 23% more housing permits in year one is now the baseline — the Mamdani plan aims to accelerate further.

**June 4 update:** City of Yes 12 months in — NYC seeing 23% more new homes. 12,000+ units in pipeline from office conversions including 3,000+ affordable. Park Slope and Prospect Heights seeing largest increases in allowable square footage under City of Yes.

**June 22 update:** City of Yes continues delivering results. ADU Program intake closed June 12 with 3,100+ applications. Q1 2026 housing permits nearly doubled vs 2025 average.

**June 23 update:** ⚡ PRIMARY ELECTION DAY — polls open 6 AM - 9 PM. Early voting has concluded; over 275,000 New Yorkers cast early ballots with Brooklyn leading at 30,000+ check-ins. Council composition will determine City of Yes implementation momentum going forward.

**June 28 update:** City of Yes housing reforms implementation tracking continues across Brooklyn. Monitor for permitting data and construction starts under new zoning framework.

**July 1 update:** ⚡ NYC Council adopted $115.9B FY2026 budget unanimously on June 30 — includes $4B capital + $1B expense for City for All housing. Mayor Mamdani's 'Block by Block' plan (May 2026) targets 200,000 new affordable + 200,000 preserved over 10 years. City of Yes (enacted December 2024) projected to enable 82,000+ homes over 15 years. Monitor Point full Council vote expected early July — test case for implementation. IBX Draft EIS expected fall/winter 2026.

public_comment.instructions: Policy adopted. Monitor individual project applications and community board proceedings for implementation progress.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: City of Yes is the single most important housing policy change in NYC in decades — and now the question is whether it actually gets built. The 80,000+ unit projection is transformative if realized. Brooklyn is ground zero for implementation, with the Atlantic Avenue plan as the flagship. As a YIMBY donor, this is where your money has the highest expected value: supporting pro-housing candidates and advocacy organizations that defend City of Yes from rollback attempts and accelerate implementation.

issues.title: COPA Reintroduced — Community Opportunity to Purchase Act Nears Passage
issues.summary:
NEW (May 22, 2026): The Community Opportunity to Purchase Act (COPA) has been reintroduced by Council Member Sandy Nurse and is nearing passage with City Council majority support. The bill would give nonprofits or certified joint ventures first dibs at buying certain distressed multifamily buildings when they go for sale — the second attempt after former Mayor Adams vetoed the original version. The modified bill includes new concessions: eligibility now only applies to buildings with 100 or fewer units whose affordability restrictions expire within two years, and buildings with 421a tax breaks are exempt. Nurse estimates COPA would have applied to just 335 of 51,000 buildings sold in 2025 — roughly 0.6% of transactions. Real estate industry opposition remains strong, but the narrower scope addresses some of the original concerns. Under Mayor Mamdani, the bill is expected to be signed into law.

**June 7 update:** COPA now has 26 council sponsors — a clear majority. Unlike the previous version vetoed by Adams, Mayor Mamdani backed COPA on the campaign trail and has reiterated support in office, meaning no veto threat. The narrowed bill applies to buildings with 100 or fewer units whose affordability restrictions expire within two years — covering only ~0.6% of annual building transactions. Council Member Nurse framed the revision as 'stronger and more targeted.' The Real Deal reports modified COPA 'nears passage despite real estate pushback.' A council vote is expected in coming weeks.

**June 8 update:** COPA has 26 council sponsors — a clear majority. No veto threat from Mayor Mamdani. Council vote expected in coming weeks. The narrowed bill applies to buildings with 100 or fewer units whose affordability restrictions expire within two years. Early voting for the June 23 primary starts June 13 (5 DAYS).

**June 9 update:** COPA reached 26 council sponsors within one week of reintroduction on May 14 — a clear majority. The reintroduced version is narrower: applies to buildings with 100 or fewer units whose affordability restrictions expire within two years. Council Member Nurse expects a hearing in fall, with passage by year's end. Mayor Mamdani publicly supports the bill. No veto threat. Early voting for the June 23 primary starts June 13 (4 DAYS) — multiple Brooklyn seats on the ballot will shape the council's housing agenda.

**June 10 update:** COPA (Intro. 905) has 26 sponsors — veto-proof majority. The 2026 version is narrower and 'stronger': targets distressed buildings with an average of 3+ open hazardous violations per year. Timeline shortened to 100 days total (45 days to express interest + 90 days to make offer, down from the vetoed version's 180 days). Backed by 200+ organizations including CLTs, tenant associations, affordable housing developers, labor unions. Mayor Mamdani supports — unlike Adams who vetoed it. Bill language still being finalized; no hearing date yet. Early voting starts June 13 (3 DAYS) — Brooklyn council seats on the ballot will shape the housing agenda.

**June 11 update:** COPA reintroduction advancing — Council Member Sandy Nurse rallied with tenants May 14 to announce the new version. Bill language still being finalized; Nurse stated 'We are in the process of finalizing the language... hoping to have something introduced very quickly.' The 2026 version targets distressed buildings — those in the Alternative Enforcement Program, on the Certificate of No Harassment list, or about to lose subsidies (421a, LIHTC). The bill gives qualified nonprofits or joint ventures a first opportunity to purchase before open market listing. Backed by 200+ organizations. Early voting starts June 13 (2 DAYS) — Brooklyn council seats on the ballot will shape the housing agenda.

**June 12 update:** COPA bill language still being finalized — no hearing date yet. The 2026 version targets distressed buildings: those in the Alternative Enforcement Program, on the Certificate of No Harassment list, or about to lose subsidies. Timeline: 100 days total (45 days to express interest + 90 days to make offer). 26 sponsors (veto-proof). Backed by 200+ organizations. Mayor Mamdani supports. ⚡ Early voting starts TOMORROW (June 13) — Brooklyn council seats on the ballot will determine whether COPA has the votes to pass and override any future veto. Primary Election Day June 23 (11 DAYS).

**June 13 update:** COPA bill language still being finalized — no hearing date yet. 26 sponsors (veto-proof). Targets distressed buildings: Alternative Enforcement Program, Certificate of No Harassment list, or expiring subsidies. ⚡ EARLY VOTING STARTS TODAY — polls open 9 AM - 5 PM. Brooklyn council seats on the ballot will determine whether COPA has the votes to pass. Last day to register in person. Primary June 23 (10 DAYS).

**June 14 update:** COPA bill language still being finalized — no hearing date yet. 26 sponsors (veto-proof). ⚡ EARLY VOTING DAY 2 — polls open 9 AM - 5 PM. Brooklyn council seats on the ballot will determine whether COPA has the votes to pass. Primary June 23 (9 DAYS). Early voting continues through June 21.

**June 15 update:** COPA bill language still being finalized — no hearing date yet. 26 sponsors (veto-proof). ⚡ EARLY VOTING DAY 3 — Monday, polls 9 AM - 5 PM; Tue-Wed extended 10 AM - 8 PM. Brooklyn council seats on the ballot determine whether COPA has the votes. Primary June 23 (8 DAYS).

**June 16 update:** COPA bill language still being finalized — no hearing date yet. 26 sponsors (veto-proof). ⚡ EARLY VOTING DAY 4 — extended hours 10 AM - 8 PM. 44,000+ voted citywide through Day 3; Brooklyn: 11,438 check-ins. Brooklyn council seats on the ballot determine whether COPA has the votes. Primary June 23 (7 DAYS).

**June 17 update:** COPA bill language still being finalized — no hearing date yet. 26 sponsors (veto-proof). ⚡ EARLY VOTING DAY 5 — 62,244 voted citywide through Day 4; Brooklyn: 16,762 check-ins. Brooklyn council seats on the ballot determine whether COPA has the votes. Primary June 23 (6 DAYS).

**June 18 update:** COPA bill language still being finalized — no hearing date yet. 26 sponsors (veto-proof). ⚡ EARLY VOTING DAY 6 — 77,000+ voted citywide through Day 5; Brooklyn: 21,675 check-ins. Turnout down 50%+ vs last year. Brooklyn council seats on the ballot determine whether COPA has the votes. Primary June 23 (5 DAYS).

**June 19 update:** Early voting Day 7 (Juneteenth). 92,000+ voted citywide through Day 6; Brooklyn 26,464. COPA bill — no hearing date set yet; 26 sponsors (veto-proof). Primary June 23 (4 DAYS) — council composition determines COPA's path.

**June 22 update:** COPA bill language still being finalized — no hearing date yet. 26 sponsors (veto-proof). Early voting final day today. Primary TOMORROW (June 23) — council composition determines COPA's path forward.

**June 23 update:** ⚡ PRIMARY ELECTION DAY — polls open 6 AM - 9 PM. Early voting has concluded; over 275,000 New Yorkers cast early ballots. COPA bill language still being finalized — no hearing date yet. 26 sponsors (veto-proof). Today's council race outcomes will determine whether COPA has the votes to pass.

**June 28 update:** COPA reintroduced with 26 sponsors — nearing veto-proof majority for passage. Monitor for full council vote.

public_comment.instructions: Bill nearing council vote. Monitor council.nyc.gov for final vote schedule.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: COPA represents a different approach to affordability than supply-side YIMBY policy — it uses community land trusts and nonprofit ownership to preserve existing affordable housing rather than building new units. The narrowed scope (0.6% of transactions) limits both its impact and the real estate industry's strongest objections. For a pragmatic housing advocate, the question is whether COPA complements or competes with supply-side approaches. The answer is probably 'complements' — preventing the loss of existing affordable units while new supply comes online through City of Yes and Atlantic Avenue rezoning.

issues.title: NYC June 23 Primary Election — Brooklyn Council and State Races
issues.governing_body: New York City Council / New York State Legislature
issues.summary:
NEW (June 3, 2026): The June 23 primary election includes multiple Brooklyn-area races. Key context: NYC's pro-housing policy trajectory — City of Yes, Atlantic Avenue rezoning (4,600 units), Block by Block ($22B housing plan), South of Prospect rezoning — depends on maintaining a pro-building council majority. Evaluate candidates on their positions regarding the small lots reform (35,000 potential units), NYCHA investment ($5.6B committed), and Construction Justice Act. The East 98th Street rezoning in East Flatbush and the Brownsville Neighborhood Community Plan are advancing, creating development-specific issues for Brooklyn district candidates to address.

**June 7 update:** ⚡ EARLY VOTING STARTS JUNE 13 — 6 DAYS AWAY. Primary Election Day is June 23. Multiple Brooklyn-area state legislative races in play including challenges in districts represented by Assembly Members Stefani Zinerman, Erik Dilan, and Steven Raga. City of Yes ADU applications: 98 received across Brooklyn, Bronx, Staten Island, and Queens — deadline June 12 (5 DAYS). East New York's rezoning continues to deliver: new construction boom WITHOUT gentrification or displacement, thanks to mandatory inclusionary housing requirements. The East 98th Street rezoning public scoping session is June 11 (4 DAYS).

**June 8 update:** ⚡ EARLY VOTING STARTS JUNE 13 — 5 DAYS AWAY. Primary Election Day is June 23 (15 DAYS). City of Yes ADU application deadline is June 12 (4 DAYS). East 98th Street rezoning public scoping session was June 11 (3 DAYS). Multiple Brooklyn-area state legislative races in play. Register to vote NOW if you haven't already.

**June 9 update:** ⚡ EARLY VOTING STARTS JUNE 13 — 4 DAYS AWAY. Early voting runs June 13-21. Primary Election Day is June 23 (14 DAYS). City of Yes ADU application deadline is June 12 (3 DAYS). East 98th Street rezoning public scoping session is June 11 (2 DAYS) at 2 PM. Key Brooklyn races: CD-7 (Velázquez seat — Reynoso, Valdez, Won competing), multiple state legislative seats. Register now if you haven't.

**June 10 update:** ⚡ EARLY VOTING STARTS JUNE 13 — 3 DAYS AWAY. June 13 is ALSO the last day to register to vote AND the last day to request an absentee/mail ballot by mail. Early voting runs June 13-21 (Sat/Sun 9-5, Mon 9-5, Tue/Wed 10-8, Thu 9-5, Fri/Juneteenth 8-4). Primary Election Day June 23 (13 DAYS). CD-7 (Velázquez seat) latest polling: Valdez (DSA) 23%, Reynoso 21%, Won 13%, 43% UNDECIDED — this race is wide open with early voting days away. Key endorsements: Reynoso has Velázquez, AG James, WFP, 32BJ SEIU; Valdez has Mayor Mamdani, UAW, Sanders, NYC DSA. East 98th Street scoping TOMORROW (June 11) at 2 PM. ADU deadline June 12 (2 DAYS).

**June 11 update:** ⚡ EARLY VOTING STARTS JUNE 13 — 2 DAYS AWAY. June 13 is ALSO the last day to register to vote AND the last day to request an absentee/mail ballot by mail. Early voting runs June 13-21 (varying hours by day). Primary Election Day June 23 (12 DAYS). CD-7 (Velázquez seat): Emerson/PIX11 poll shows Valdez (DSA) 23%, Reynoso 21%, Won 13%, 43% UNDECIDED — race wide open with early voting imminent. Key dynamic: voters under 40 favor Valdez 33%-15%, voters over 50 favor Reynoso 27%-13%. Reynoso has Velázquez, AG James, WFP, 32BJ SEIU, broad labor wall; Valdez has Mayor Mamdani, UAW, Sanders, NYC DSA. CBS described race as testing 'power of Mamdani's endorsement.' ADU deadline TOMORROW (June 12). East 98th Street scoping session TODAY at 2 PM.

**June 12 update:** ⚡ EARLY VOTING STARTS TOMORROW (June 13). June 13 is ALSO the last day to register to vote in person AND the last day to request an absentee/mail ballot by mail. Early voting runs June 13-21; polls open 6 AM - 9 PM. Primary Election Day June 23 (11 DAYS). CD-7 (Velázquez seat): prediction markets now show Valdez (DSA) at 77% vs Reynoso at 17% — a commanding lead driven by strong fundraising ($750K+ through March), Mayor Mamdani's endorsement, and DSA network mobilization. Emerson/PIX11 poll had Valdez 23%, Reynoso 21%, Won 13%, 43% undecided — but early vote dynamics may favor the candidate with the strongest ground operation. ADU deadline TODAY (see housing update).

**June 13 update:** ⚡ EARLY VOTING STARTS TODAY — polls open 9 AM - 5 PM. TODAY is also the LAST DAY to register to vote in person AND the last day to request an absentee/mail ballot. Early voting runs through June 21. Primary Election Day June 23 (10 DAYS). You MUST go to your assigned early voting poll site — it may differ from your Election Day location (check vote.nyc). CD-7 (Velázquez seat): prediction markets now show Valdez (DSA) at 80% (up from 77%), Reynoso at 19%. Emerson/PIX11 poll: Valdez 23%, Reynoso 21%, Won 13% — but 43% undecided. Clear age divide: under-40 voters break for Valdez 33%-15%; over-50 break for Reynoso 27%-13%. Strong DSA ground operation may drive early vote turnout.

**June 14 update:** ⚡ EARLY VOTING DAY 2 — polls open 9 AM - 5 PM (Sunday). Go to your assigned early voting poll site — it may differ from your Election Day location (check vote.nyc). Early voting continues through June 21. Primary Election Day June 23 (9 DAYS). CD-7 (Velázquez seat): prediction markets show Valdez (DSA) holding at 80%, Reynoso at 22%. Emerson/PIX11 poll: Valdez 23%, Reynoso 21%, Won 13% — but 43% undecided. Strong DSA ground operation targeting early vote turnout. Tomorrow (Monday June 15): polls open 9 AM - 5 PM. Tuesday-Wednesday: extended hours 10 AM - 8 PM.

**June 15 update:** ⚡ EARLY VOTING DAY 3 — Monday, polls open 9 AM - 5 PM. Go to your assigned early voting poll site (check vote.nyc — it may differ from your Election Day location). Tomorrow and Wednesday: extended hours 10 AM - 8 PM. Early voting continues through June 21. Primary Election Day June 23 (8 DAYS). CD-7 (Velázquez seat): prediction markets show Valdez (DSA) holding at 80%, Reynoso at 22%; Emerson/PIX11 poll had Valdez 23%, Reynoso 21%, Won 13% — but 43% undecided. Strong DSA ground operation targeting early vote turnout. Key endorsements for Valdez: Mayor Mamdani and Sen. Bernie Sanders.

**June 16 update:** ⚡ EARLY VOTING DAY 4 — extended hours 10 AM - 8 PM (Monday through Wednesday). 44,000+ New Yorkers voted through Day 3 citywide; Brooklyn: 11,438 check-ins through Day 3. Early voting continues through June 21. Primary Election Day June 23 (7 DAYS). CD-7 (Velázquez seat): prediction markets show Valdez (DSA) holding at 80%, Reynoso at 22%; Emerson/PIX11 poll had Valdez 23%, Reynoso 21%, Won 13% — 43% undecided. Strong age divide: voters under 40 favor Valdez 33-15%, voters over 50 break for Reynoso 27-13%. Key endorsements for Valdez: Mayor Mamdani and Sen. Bernie Sanders.

**June 17 update:** ⚡ EARLY VOTING DAY 5 — polls 10 AM - 8 PM (Tuesday). Through Day 4: 62,244 total voters citywide (+18,167 from Day 3); Brooklyn: 16,762 check-ins (+5,324 from Day 3). Manhattan leads at 26,607, Queens 11,755, Bronx 5,774, Staten Island 1,346. Early voting continues through June 21. Primary Election Day June 23 (6 DAYS). CD-7 (Velázquez seat): prediction markets now show Valdez (DSA) at 83% (up from 80%), Reynoso at 19%. Strong age divide persists: voters under 40 favor Valdez 33-15%, over 50 break for Reynoso 27-13%. Key endorsements for Valdez: Mayor Mamdani and Sen. Bernie Sanders. 43% of voters still undecided in Emerson poll.

**June 18 update:** ⚡ EARLY VOTING DAY 6 — polls 8 AM - 4 PM (Wednesday). Through Day 5: 77,000+ total voters citywide (+14,756 from Day 4); Brooklyn: 21,675 check-ins (+4,913 from Day 4). Manhattan leads at 32,738, Queens 14,530, Bronx 7,262, Staten Island 1,625. Turnout down 50%+ compared to last year. Early voting continues through June 21. Primary Election Day June 23 (5 DAYS). CD-7 (Velázquez seat): prediction markets show Valdez at 79% (down from 83%), Reynoso at 21%. Emerson poll: Valdez 23%, Reynoso 21%, Won 13%, 43% undecided. Strong age divide persists: under-40 voters favor Valdez 33-15%, over-50 break for Reynoso 27-13%.

**June 19 update:** ⚡ EARLY VOTING DAY 7 (Juneteenth) — polls open with extended hours. Through Day 6 (June 18): 92,000+ total voters citywide; Brooklyn: 26,464 check-ins (+4,789 from Day 5). Manhattan 38,413, Queens 17,265, Bronx 8,323, Staten Island 1,882. Early voting continues through Saturday June 21. Primary Day June 23 (4 DAYS). CD-7 (Velázquez seat): Valdez still favored on prediction markets. Emerson poll: Valdez 23%, Reynoso 21%, Won 13%, 43% undecided — race remains fluid with high undecideds.

**June 22 update:** ⚡ EARLY VOTING FINAL DAY (Day 10) — last chance before Primary Day. 110,000+ estimated citywide early voters through Day 9. ⚡ PRIMARY ELECTION IS TOMORROW (June 23) — polls open 6 AM - 9 PM. Go to your assigned Election Day poll site (check vote.nyc). CD-7 (Velázquez seat): Valdez still favored on prediction markets; Emerson poll had 43% undecided — outcome remains uncertain. Brooklyn council seats on the ballot will shape housing policy for the next four years.

**June 23 update:** ⚡ PRIMARY ELECTION DAY — polls open 6 AM - 9 PM. Early voting has concluded with over 275,000 New Yorkers casting early ballots. Brooklyn led early voting with 30,000+ check-ins. Go to your assigned Election Day poll site (check vote.nyc). CD-7 (Velázquez open seat) is the marquee race: Assembly Member Claire Valdez (DSA) leads at 81% on prediction markets vs Brooklyn Borough President Antonio Reynoso (21%). Council Member Julie Won and newcomer Vichal Kumar also running. Valdez endorsed by Mayor Mamdani; Reynoso endorsed by retiring Rep. Velázquez. Results expected tonight. Multiple Brooklyn City Council seats on the ballot will shape housing policy for the next four years.

**June 28 update:** NYC June 23 primary results being certified. Multiple Brooklyn races determined — ranked choice voting tabulation completed. Monitor for general election matchups and campaign dynamics.

public_comment.instructions: Check nycvotes.org/whats-on-the-ballot for your specific races. Primary is June 23.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: The June 23 primary determines which candidates advance for Brooklyn council seats. With the City of Yes framework in place and Speaker Menin's small lots reform in development, the next council's composition will determine whether NYC's historic housing momentum continues or stalls. Evaluate every candidate on their housing record and reform positions.
